name: NMS2 (nitrate mineral salts) medium + 10 mL 1M carbonate buffer per liter (PMC10466089)
note: The paper does NOT describe the composition of NMS2 medium. It names it and defers the formulation entirely to cited reference 20; the only medium-specific detail given in the paper text is that 10 mL of 1 M carbonate buffer was added per liter. NMS2 (a nitrate mineral salts medium for the aerobic gammaproteobacterial methanotroph M. buryatense) IS a real, well-established growth medium in the methanotroph literature (Puri/Kalyuzhnaya lineage: contains nitrate N-source, phosphate buffer, MgSO4, CaCl2, Fe-EDTA, trace elements, NaCl/NaHCO3 for the alkaliphile), but none of that composition is present or reconstructable from THIS paper, so it cannot be verified or corrected against the source. The auto-extracted record is broken: it reduced the whole medium to O2 plus an unresolved 'carbonate buffer (1M)', with fewer than 3 real components and no minerals despite the name — not a usable formulation. Organism is an obligate aerobic methanotroph, so oxygen = aerobic. Recommend not publishing a formulation from this paper; source NMS2 from its primary reference instead.
evidence: Methylotuvimicrobium buryatense 5GB1C and Methylotuvimicrobium alcaliphilum 20Z(R) were grown in NMS2 medium (20). ... Per liter of NMS2 medium, 10 mL 1M carbonate buffer was added.

name: Sulfate-free artificial seawater (SFASW) or 0.2 um filtered natural seawater (FSW), sponge-explant incubation +/- taurine (PMC10356861)
note: REJECTED as a microbial growth medium. This entry describes seawater (sulfate-free artificial seawater SFASW, or 0.2 um-filtered natural seawater FSW) used to incubate WHOLE LIVE SPONGE EXPLANTS of Ianthella basta for holobiont physiology experiments (nitrification / taurine dissimilation / sulfate production). No prokaryote is cultured in isolation; the organism of interest, 'Taurinisymbion ianthellae', is an uncultivated symbiont. Taurine is a substrate/13C15N-tracer amendment (1.6 mM total), not a growth-medium carbon source. Additionally, the true SFASW (sulfate-free) composition is given only in the Supplementary Note, which is NOT present in the available full-text XML, so it cannot be reconstructed. The only seawater recipe in the main text (per L: 26.4 g NaCl, 6.8 g MgSO4*7H2O, 5.7 g MgCl2*6H2O, 1.47 g CaCl2*2H2O, 0.66 g KCl, 0.2 g KH2PO4, 0.19 g NaHCO3) is a SULFATE-CONTAINING artificial seawater used to dilute taurine standards, i.e. explicitly NOT the sulfate-free SFASW named in this entry. The stored formulation (only O2 + 'taurine amendment', flagged 'fewer than 3 real components') is a substrate list, not a medium. Recommend removing this record from the public growth-media DB.
evidence: Live sponge explants were transferred to sulfate-free artificial seawater (SFASW; see Supplementary Note for media details) and rinsed three times to remove as much sulfate from seawater as possible. For all incubation experiments, [I. basta explants were incubated in] filtered seawater (FSW) or SFASW, and with or without taurine added at different time points and concentrations.

name: Anaerobic bicarbonate-buffered mineral medium (H2/HCO3- fed) with isoprene; cell-suspension microcosm (PMC10134865)
note: This paper does NOT define a mineral-medium formulation. The anaerobic bicarbonate-buffered mineral medium is cited to reference 23 ('as described previously'), not composed here. The text contains zero mineral salts, buffers-by-name, vitamins, or trace elements (grep for NaCl/KCl/NH4Cl/MgSO4/MgCl2/CaCl2/KH2PO4/Na2HPO4/FeCl/B12/yeast extract/selenite/tungstate all = 0). The only medium-related quantities given are substrate/redox additions: H2 headspace (~5-7 ×10^4 Pa), HCO3- (40-60 mM, buffer + C source for the acetogen), isoprene (1 mM, terminal electron acceptor), and titanium citrate (1 mM, reductant). The auto-extracted entry is therefore a substrate/electron-donor-acceptor list mislabelled as a defined mineral medium; it cannot be corrected to a full formulation from THIS paper because the composition is not present. Organism (Acetobacterium wieringae, homoacetogen using isoprene as anaerobic electron acceptor) and conditions (N2 flushing, titanium-citrate reductant, anaerobic chamber) are unambiguously anaerobic. Recommend rejecting this record or sourcing the true composition from primary reference 23 (the enrichment-culture cultivation paper).
evidence: Isoprene-reducing biomass was grown on H2/HCO3-/±isoprene, as described previously (23). ... Cells were washed in minimal medium containing 1 mM titanium citrate (23) ... H2 (7 × 10^4 Pa), HCO3- (60 mM), and isoprene (1 mM) were added, and cells were incubated at 30°C.

name: Defined artificial seawater (ESAW, modified) with P. tricornutum exudates + 13C/15N-labeled algal DOM (PMC10499878)
note: REJECTED as a defined growth medium. The entry name ('Defined artificial seawater (ESAW, modified) with P. tricornutum exudates + 13C/15N-labeled algal DOM') conflates three unrelated things and none of them constitute a chemically-defined, GEM-mappable formulation: (1) The actual co-culture medium was NOT ESAW but F/2 made with Instant Ocean sea salts (35 g/L) + 880 µM nitrate — a proprietary, complex, chemically-undefined marine sea-salt mixture. (2) ESAW is named only once, for a separate low-nutrient 13C-bicarbonate anaplerotic experiment, and the paper gives NO ESAW composition (no salt/vitamin/trace-metal recipe), so it cannot be corrected to a full defined list. (3) The '13C/15N-labeled algal DOM' is a solid-phase-extracted isotope TRACER (labeled P. tricornutum spent-medium organic matter) added for nanoSIMS, and 'P. tricornutum exudates/spent medium' is undefined complex organic matter — neither is a defined medium ingredient. The current 3-component skeleton (O2 + nitrate + phosphate) is an auto-extraction artifact, not the paper's formulation. Oxygen: aerobic (oxic marine surface-water diatom-heterotroph co-cultures under a 14/10 h light regime; the ESAW+bicarbonate experiment is likewise aerobic). Recommend removing this record or reclassifying it as a complex/undefined marine medium (Instant Ocean F/2), not a defined seawater medium.
evidence: The seawater (F/2) medium was prepared using Instant Ocean salts (35 g/L) with 880 µM nitrate ... we tested the anapleurotic activity ... by incubating the strains in autotrophic medium (artificial seawater ESAW) with no added carbon plus 13C-labeled bicarbonate (1.5 mM added) for one week.

name: Undiluted reactor culture (RP1) in anaerobic serum bottle, spiked with sodium propionate (PMC10579422)
note: This is NOT a defined/formulated growth medium. It is an undiluted, undefined enrichment reactor culture (a live thermophilic high-ammonia syntrophic microbial community plus residual BM feed) transferred into a serum bottle and spiked with sodium propionate for a batch kinetics assay (B01-B12). It is an experimental incubation setup mislabelled as a medium (the auto-extract itself flagged 'complex/undefined base', n_components=1). Reject. If a defined medium is wanted, the underlying feed medium is captured by the sibling record (bicarbonate-buffered BM + NH4Cl + sodium propionate). The current record's aerobic assumption is also wrong - this is strictly anaerobic (52 C, N2-flushed, syntrophic biogas community).
evidence: anaerobic batch assays were prepared by transferring 0.5 L of the enrichment culture from the continuously propionate- and acetate-fed reactors after 630 days of operation directly to sterile anaerobic serum bottles (1 L) under constant N2 flushing ... sodium propionate or sodium acetate was added to the batch culture, with the intention to reach a final concentration of 30 mM

name: Freshwater lake sediment microcosm (Lianhuachi Lake, Beijing) spiked with 13C-bicarbonate + 15N-nitrate (PMC11344931)
note: This is NOT a defined (or even semi-defined) growth medium. The target organism, 'Candidatus Magnetobacterium' sp. LHC-1 (a Nitrospirota magnetotactic bacterium), is UNCULTURED — the paper states cultivation of Nitrospirota MTB has never been achieved. What is described is a natural freshwater lake-sediment microcosm (native Lianhuachi Lake sediment + lake water, chemically undefined) held in the dark for recovery, then spiked with stable-isotope tracers (Na15NO3, NaH13CO3) purely for NanoSIMS single-cell isotope-uptake measurement. Cells were magnetically enriched directly from sediment and analyzed in situ; they were never grown in any formulated medium. There is no reproducible component formulation to curate: the bulk is an undefined environmental matrix (sediment slurry, pH 7.1, salinity 0.52 ppt). The auto-extraction correctly flagged 'fewer than 3 real components' and 'complex/undefined base — extracts not BiGG-mappable'. This should be removed from a growth-media database. Oxygen: MTB of this type inhabit the oxic-anoxic transition zone (microaerophilic); the sediment microcosm has an O2 gradient and the organism performs nitrate reduction and CO2 fixation, so 'facultative' best reflects the undefined gradient environment (closest schema option to microaerophilic).
evidence: Each of the 15 subsamples contained about 17 mL of sediment and 8 mL of lake water... Then, the isotopes incubation started by adding 112.5 μL of 1-M Na15NO3 (98 atom% 15N, Sigma-Aldrich) and 50 μL of 1-M NaH13CO3 (98 atom% 13C, Sigma-Aldrich) into each subsample.

name: K3 defined minimal medium with glucose (PMC11097648)
note: This paper (mSystems, E. coli trpC/tyrA auxotroph cross-feeding consortium) NAMES the medium as 'K3 defined minimal medium' but delegates its full composition to citation (5) and does NOT enumerate any salts, N source, phosphate, buffer, vitamins, or trace elements anywhere in the text (0 hits for Na2HPO4/KH2PO4/NH4Cl/MgSO4/CaCl2/phosphate/ammonium/thiamine/vitamin/FeCl/EDTA). Only the carbon source (glucose, 4.5 g/L batch) and a selection antibiotic (kanamycin, 33 µg/L — not a nutrient) are specified in-paper. 'K3' is not a standard named base (unlike M9/LB/MOPS), so the mineral composition cannot be reconstructed without inventing it. The current DB record (glucose + O2 only) is chemically incomplete for a minimal medium and should NOT be published as a K3 formulation. Recommend retrieving the recipe from the cited source before including. Culture was aerobic shake-flask (37°C, 225 rpm); E. coli is facultative but grown aerobically here.
evidence: The washed cells were resuspended and transferred to 150 mL flasks carrying 25 mL of K3 defined minimal medium (5) containing glucose and 33 µg/L kanamycin and cultivated at 37°C and 225 rpm ... For batch mode, 4.5 g/L glucose was supplied in the culture medium.

name: semi-synthetic media simulating sugarcane molasses (SM) (PMC11193817)
note: PMC11193817 (Nat Commun 2024, de Oliveira Lino et al.) does NOT provide the formulation of this medium. It only NAMES and USES a 'semi-synthetic medium simulating sugarcane molasses (SM)' and cites its recipe to reference 26 = Lino FS, Basso TO, Sommer MOA, 'A synthetic medium to simulate sugarcane molasses', Biotechnol. Biofuels 2018, 11, 221 (doi:10.1186/s13068-018-1221-x). The full composition (salts, N source, vitamins, trace elements, organic acids such as aconitate) is deferred to that reference and to this paper's Supplementary Tables 4-5, neither of which is present in the fetched fulltext XML. The ONLY composition detail this article itself states is the carbohydrate working concentration: total sugar 18 g/L composed of sucrose, glucose and fructose (a 10x dilution of the SM stock). SM is a real, chemically semi-defined medium, so this is not a hallucination or a mislabelled substrate list (not 'rejected'); however its formulation cannot be established or verified from THIS paper (hence 'not_found'). RECOMMENDATION for the database: re-source the full SM recipe from Lino et al. 2018 Biotechnol Biofuels (ref 26) plus PMC11193817 Supplementary Tables 4-5 before publishing a composition; do not publish the current near-empty record as a verified formulation. Oxygen: cultivations of lactic acid bacteria (Limosilactobacillus fermentum etc.) and S. cerevisiae PE-2 were done statically overnight (low-O2), and growth-rate assays under mild agitation; the LAB involved are aerotolerant/facultative, so oxygen is best labelled facultative rather than strictly aerobic or anaerobic.
evidence: The media used is a semi-synthetic media, able to simulate sugarcane molasses based media (SM)26. ... cells were diluted in SM diluted in sterile Milli-Q H2O (10x, final sugar concentration of 18 g/L). ... The media composition utilized in the microplate experiments can be found in the Supplementary Tables 4 and 5.

name: In-vivo honey bee (Apis mellifera) ileum; host diet 99% [U-13C6] glucose vs naturally abundant glucose in sugar water (PMC11343714)
note: Not a defined bacterial growth medium. This entry describes an IN VIVO host condition (the honey bee ileum) plus the host DIET (sugar water: sucrose +/- [U-13C6]glucose) used for isotope pulse-chase labelling. It is not a medium in which S. alvi is cultured; indeed S. alvi cannot metabolize saccharides (no functional glycolysis) and grows on host-secreted organic acids, not on this sugar water. A sugar-water diet mislabelled as a growth medium -> reject. Should be removed from the media database (or reclassified as an in vivo tracer condition, not a growth medium).
evidence: we enriched newly emerged MF bees in 13C by feeding them 100% [U-13C6] glucose for 4 days after emergence ... then switching their diet to naturally abundant, 98.9% 12C glucose. ... we fed MF bees for 6 days with 45% of the dietary sucrose replaced by 100% [U-13C6] glucose.

name: Pi3% nitrate mineral medium (methane, Ca/no-lanthanide) (PMC11748545)
note: This paper does NOT define the composition of the 'Pi3% nitrate mineral medium'. It only names the medium and cites its formulation to reference 25 (Collins DA, Kalyuzhnaya MG, 2018, 'Navigating methane metabolism', Methods in Enzymology, doi:10.1016/bs.mie.2018.10.010). No salt, nitrate, phosphate, calcium, lanthanide, or trace-element amounts are given anywhere in this article — the auto-extraction itself flagged the true composition as living 'in ref 25'. Verdict is not_found because the source paper does not describe/define this medium's formulation; the maintainer must curate it from Collins & Kalyuzhnaya 2018, not from PMC11748545. Context the paper does supply: organism is the aerobic alkaliphilic methanotroph M. alcaliphilum 20Z^R; growth at pH 8.7–9, 30°C; carbon/energy source is methane (or methanol 0.2%) with a 20:80 methane:air headspace (aerobic — O2 required for methane monooxygenase); nitrogen source is nitrate ('nitrate mineral media'); phosphate-buffered ('Pi3%'); this specific variant is the Ca-supplemented, no-lanthanide condition. Amounts are unknown from this source, so no numeric formulation can be honestly asserted here. The current stored formulation (only O2 + methane) is not a usable medium definition regardless.
evidence: M. alcaliphilum 20Z^R strains were routinely grown in Pi3% nitrate mineral media supplemented with methanol (0.2%, vol/vol) or methane as described previously (25). Methane (25–50 mL) was introduced into 125–250 mL vials ... to keep a methane:air ratio of 20:80 in headspace.

name: Basal anaerobic (BA) medium + 0.2 g/L yeast extract (PMC11844248)
note: The paper names and uses 'basal anaerobic (BA) medium + 0.2 g/L yeast extract' but its actual composition is NOT in the fulltext XML — it is defined only in 'Supplementary Document 1' and 'Supplementary Table 1', which are not available here. Therefore the true full formulation cannot be reconstructed or confirmed from the paper. The current auto-extracted entry is INCORRECT: it is a fabricated decomposition of the words 'yeast extract' into 45 generic components (20 amino acids, B-vitamins, nucleosides, trace metals) with no concentrations, all tagged derived_from=yeast extract. It omits the entire BA basal medium (mineral salts, phosphate + bicarbonate buffer, anaerobic reducing agent, defined trace-element/vitamin solution) and the real carbon sources (acetate and CO2/H2), and it invents component identities/amounts not stated in the paper. Recommend rejecting the current 45-component list and re-curating from the paper's Supplementary Document 1 / Supplementary Table 1. Context: thermophilic (55 C) anaerobic methanogenic enrichment dominated by Methanothermobacter wolfeii; strictly anaerobic. Components below are only those genuinely supported by the accessible fulltext.
evidence: Each reactor consisted of a 1 L glass bottle with a 350 ml working volume of BA medium supplemented with limited addition of yeast extract (0.2 g/L final concentration) ... The culture was grown in basal anaerobic (BA) medium and fed with pure CO2 and H2 in 1:4 volumetric ratio.

name: mPGT (semidefined, iron-limited medium) (PMC11725911)
note: This paper (Mahoney et al., PNAS 2024, Corynebacterium diphtheriae hemoglobin/heme uptake) NAMES the semidefined iron-limited medium 'mPGT' but does NOT provide its composition — it defers the recipe to reference 93 ('previously described (93)'). Therefore the full formulation cannot be verified or reconstructed from THIS source paper. The only medium-related quantities THIS paper specifies are three supplements/conditions: FeCl3 at 0.25 uM (low-iron condition), kanamycin at 50 ug/mL (selection), and purified human hemoglobin (MP Biomedical) added as an iron/heme source in the growth assays. The auto-extracted formulation is wrong: it lists 'O2' and 'hemoglobin (Hb)' as the medium, but hemoglobin is the iron source under study (a variable substrate), not a base medium component, and O2 was never asserted. The mPGT base (salts, carbon source, N source, buffer) is undefined in this paper. Recommend sourcing composition from reference 93 before publishing any formulation; do not use O2+hemoglobin as the recipe. C. diphtheriae grown aerobically.
evidence: Iron-limited growth assays were done in a semidefined medium, mPGT, which has been previously described (93). Iron chloride (FeCl3) was added at 0.25 uM for a low iron growth condition, and kanamycin was used at 50 ug/mL.

name: Oligotrophic groundwater microcosm, 'Defined' treatment (chemically defined organic compounds), no rich media/vitamins/trace elements added (PMC12577380)
note: NOT a defined growth medium. The 'Defined' treatment is natural oligotrophic groundwater from the Hainich CZE (wells H41 oxic / H52 anoxic) amended with ONE chemically-defined organic carbon SUBSTRATE (veratric acid OR cellulose OR starch). The word 'Defined' refers to the defined organic substrate, not a defined medium — the base is undefined natural groundwater of variable composition (~1-2 mg/L DOC, ~70-90 mg/L TIC, pH 7.2-7.3). No salts, buffers, N source amounts, or supplement concentrations are given in the main text (they reside in Table S1 / Supplementary Information, not in the fulltext). This is an in-situ groundwater microcosm / substrate list mislabelled as a growth medium; it is not BiGG-mappable and cannot be enumerated as a formulation. Reject. Oxygen: treatments span both oxic (H41) and anoxic (H52) conditions, hence facultative.
evidence: "All incubations were conducted in oligotrophic groundwater with minimal supplements, avoiding the use of rich media, vitamins, or trace elements. ... 'Defined' treatments included chemically defined organic compounds like veratric acid, cellulose, or starch"

name: medium (PMC13001810)
note: PMC13001810 (Xia 2026, Mol Biol Evol) is a purely computational study of codon optimization in ribosomal-protein genes; it performs no culturing and defines no growth medium. The only culture reference is a secondhand citation (Kashiwagi et al. 1989) noting that putrescine at 100 ug/mL was added to E. coli mutant (MA261/KK101) cultures to stimulate RP S1 synthesis. The auto-extracted entry (O2 + putrescine, flagged 'fewer than 3 real components') is a lone supplement mislabelled as a medium: no base, no carbon/N source, no salts, no buffer. Not a real defined growth medium -> reject.
evidence: Addition of putrescine (a polyamine) at 100 μg/mL to the culture stimulated the synthesis of RP S1 markedly but had little effect on other RPs (Kashiwagi et al. 1989).

name: EBPR sequencing batch reactor (SBR3), acetate-fed; carbon-uptake assays with acetate/lactate/succinate (PMC12766714)
note: REJECTED as a growth-medium record. This is not a defined (or even a described complex) growth medium in the paper's full text. 'SBR3' is a mixed-culture activated-sludge enrichment sequencing batch reactor inoculated with activated sludge from a Guangzhou WWTP and fed acetate, operated on anaerobic-aerobic EBPR cycles; it was used for carbon-uptake assays with acetate/lactate/succinate. The fulltext gives NO medium formulation (no salts, buffers, N/P source, trace elements, or synthetic-wastewater recipe) and explicitly defers all reactor operating conditions to the Supplementary Information, which is not present in this XML. The only 'defined minimal medium supplemented with acetate and inorganic ions' mentioned is an in-silico COBRApy modeling medium, not the reactor feed. The current auto-extracted entry is effectively just O2 plus an uncovered 'acetate' substrate (flagged 'fewer than 3 real components'), which is a substrate/reactor label mislabelled as a growth medium. Oxygen marked facultative because the system alternates anaerobic feast / aerobic phases; the confirmed carbon uptake (acetate/lactate/succinate) occurs under the anaerobic phase. Recommend removing this record or replacing it only after retrieving the full feed recipe from the paper's SI.
evidence: SBR3 ... was seeded with activated sludge from the same WWTP in Guangzhou with acetate as a carbon source. Anaerobic-aerobic cycle experiments were conducted to evaluate the ability of Ca. Accumulibacter to utilize lactate and succinate. ... Detailed reactor operating conditions, anaerobic-aerobic cycle protocols, sample collection procedures, and sequencing methodologies for all these reactors are provided in the SI.

name: SN medium (Waterbury et al. recipe), coastal seawater base (PMC13012070)
note: This paper does NOT define the composition of SN medium. It only states that Synechococcus was cultured in 'SN media prepared according to Waterbury et al.' (reference 55) using a natural 0.2-um filtered coastal seawater base (Vineyard Sound, MA). No salts, nutrients, trace elements, vitamins, or amounts are given anywhere in the text or tables of PMC13012070 — the recipe lives in the cited Waterbury et al. paper, not here. SN is a real, defined cyanobacterial enrichment medium (added to a natural-seawater base), so it is not 'rejected'; but because THIS paper supplies no formulation, the honest verdict is not_found. The current extraction is a placeholder (only 'O2' plus an unmapped 'coastal seawater' line, flagged 'fewer than 3 real components' / 'not manually verified') and cannot be verified or corrected from this source. Recovering a real recipe would require sourcing Waterbury et al. 1986, which is outside this paper. Oxygen = aerobic: the organism (Synechococcus) is an oxygenic photoautotroph grown in the light.
evidence: Synechococcus was grown in SN media prepared according to Waterbury et al. (55), all with a base of 0.2-um filtered coastal seawater collected from Vineyard Sound, MA.

name: SO medium (SN recipe omitting NaNO3, 75% Sargasso seawater base diluted with MilliQ) (PMC13012070)
note: This paper does NOT define the composition of SO medium. It only states that Crocosphaera watsonii was grown in 'SO media, which followed the SN media recipe but omitted NaNO3, with a base of 75% Sargasso seawater (diluted to 75% with MilliQ).' Since SO is defined only as 'SN recipe minus NaNO3', and this paper never gives the SN recipe (it cites Waterbury et al., ref 55), no formulation can be grounded in PMC13012070 -> not_found. The current extraction is worse than a placeholder: it lists NaNO3 (as na1 + no3 exchanges) as a component, but the paper explicitly states SO medium OMITS NaNO3 — so the one non-trivial component in the extraction is exactly backwards. It also carries only 'O2' and an unmapped '75% Sargasso seawater' base and is flagged 'not manually verified'. Oxygen = aerobic: C. watsonii is an oxygenic diazotrophic cyanobacterium grown in the light.
evidence: C. watsonii was grown in SO media, which followed the SN media recipe but omitted NaNO3, with a base of 75% Sargasso seawater (diluted to 75% with MilliQ).

name: soil slurry microcosm, 0x MWM (mineral water medium, no added basic salts; EC ~60 uS/cm) (PMC13037477)
note: REJECTED as a defined growth medium. This entity is a SOIL SLURRY MICROCOSM: 3 g fresh (grassland/agricultural) soil + 150 ml MWM incubated at 25C in the dark. It is an environmental incubation dominated by an undefined complex soil matrix, not a chemically defined/formulated growth medium usable for GEM constraints. The '0x MWM' variant explicitly contains NO added basic salts (EC ~60 uS/cm); only supplements + soil. The defined MWM base (mineral water medium) supplements are: KH2PO4, NaHCO3, HEPES, pyruvic acid, trace elements, and ferric sodium EDTA, with NH4Cl (100 uM) added as the oxidizable substrate; exact concentrations are ONLY in Supplementary Table S1, which is not present in the fulltext XML, so amounts cannot be verified. The current extraction (O2 + ammonia + 'fresh soil') is also wrong/incomplete regardless. Organism scope is ammonia oxidizers (AOA/AOB/CMX), which are obligate aerobes (O2-dependent nitrification). Recommend removing from the database; if retained, it must be labelled a soil microcosm, not a medium, and the full MWM salt composition retrieved from Supplementary Table S1.
evidence: Soil slurry microcosms were set up in 250-ml polystyrene culture flasks, each containing 3 g of fresh soil mixed with 150 ml of 0x, 1x, or 10x MWM, with triplicate samples for each condition.

name: soil slurry microcosm, 10x MWM (mineral water medium; EC ~780 uS/cm) (PMC13037477)
note: REJECTED as a defined growth medium, same rationale as the 0x variant. Soil slurry microcosm = 3 g fresh soil + 150 ml 10x MWM, EC ~780 uS/cm. It is an environmental incubation dominated by undefined soil, not a formulated medium. The ONLY difference from the 1x/0x variants is that the MWM basic salts (monovalent + divalent cations) are at 10-fold concentration; the defined supplement set is identical (KH2PO4, NaHCO3, HEPES, pyruvic acid, trace elements, ferric sodium EDTA) plus NH4Cl 100 uM as substrate. Exact salt identities and concentrations are only in Supplementary Table S1, absent from the fulltext XML, so a numeric formulation cannot be verified. Current extraction (O2 + ammonia + 'fresh soil') is wrong/incomplete. Ammonia oxidizers are aerobic. Recommend removal or relabelling as a microcosm; full salts must come from Supplementary Table S1.
evidence: For comparison, soil slurry microcosms prepared with 10x MWM had an EC value of ~780 uS/cm, corresponding to the 90th percentile of soil ECs. ... Varying salinity levels were achieved by preparing different strengths of MWM through the adjustment of monovalent and divalent cation concentrations.

name: soil slurry microcosm, 1x MWM (mineral water medium; EC ~120 uS/cm) (PMC13037477)
note: REJECTED as a defined growth medium, same rationale as the other two variants. Soil slurry microcosm = 3 g fresh soil + 150 ml 1x MWM, EC ~120-128 uS/cm. Undefined-soil-dominated environmental incubation, not a formulated medium. Differs from 0x/10x only in the concentration of MWM basic salts (monovalent/divalent cations at 1x). Defined supplements: KH2PO4, NaHCO3, HEPES, pyruvic acid, trace elements, ferric sodium EDTA; NH4Cl 100 uM as substrate. Exact salt composition only in Supplementary Table S1, not in fulltext XML, so numeric formulation is unverifiable. Current extraction (O2 + ammonia + 'fresh soil') is wrong/incomplete. Ammonia oxidizers are aerobic. Recommend removal or relabelling; retrieve full salts from Supplementary Table S1.
evidence: The ECs of the soil slurry microcosms prepared with 0x (without basic salts; EC ~60 uS/cm) and 1x MWM (EC ~120 uS/cm) were comparable to those observed in low-salinity soil environments... After all supplements were added, the EC of 1x MWM was measured at 128 uS/cm.

name: CDMG agar, co-culture spot 2.5 cm from lactate producer LM (PMC12816087)

The underlying medium is plain CDMG agar, already represented by the entry 'complexlit_PMC12816087_cdmg_chemically_defined_medium_cdm_2_glucose'. name: Rich medium (mGAM / WCA / Schaedler) with and without supplemental mucin (Sigma M1778, type III) (PMC13236585)
note: Not a real single growth medium. This entry is an auto-extraction artifact that conflates THREE distinct, separately-sold commercial complex broths (mGAM = modified Gifu Anaerobic Medium, HyServe 05433; WCA = Wilkins-Chalgren Anaerobe broth, Oxoid CM0643; Schaedler broth, Carl Roth 5772.1) into one lumped 'medium.' The paper explicitly calls these 'three undefined media' and gives NO chemical formulation for any of them; they are used as alternative rich bases for different species, not as one combined medium. The current record is also mislabelled aerobic/facultative with only O2 as a component, whereas all cultivation in the paper was strictly anaerobic (37 C, vinyl anaerobic chamber, COY). Because it is not a single defined medium and the paper defines no composition, it should be removed/split rather than formulated. If retained, WCA is captured by the sibling record; mGAM and Schaedler broth would each need their own manufacturer-formula entries.
evidence: Different media used in experiments for cultivation of individual species belong to three undefined media: mGAM (modified Gifu anaerobic medium broth, HyServe; 05433), WCA (Wilkinson-Chalgren anaerobic broth, Oxoid; CM0643) and Schaedler broth (Carl Roth; 5772.1).

name: glucose minimal medium (chemostat), as reported for E. coli K-12 MG1655 by Taymaz-Nikerel et al. 2010 and used/recalculated in this paper (PMC3261703)
note: PMC3261703 is the iJO1366 E. coli reconstruction paper (Orth et al., 2011). It does NOT report the composition of the Taymaz-Nikerel et al. 2010 chemostat glucose minimal medium — that citation is used only to recalculate GAM/NGAM maintenance values; the actual medium formulation lives in the cited Taymaz-Nikerel paper, not here. The only glucose 'minimal media' referenced in this paper are (a) in-silico FBA setups (glucose exchange lower bound -8 or -10, O2 -18.5, inorganic ions -1000 mmol gDW^-1 h^-1) which are modeling constraints, not a physical medium, and (b) physical 'glucose M9 minimal medium' in the Keio knockout screen with no salt concentrations given ('washed twice with 200 ul of 1x M9 salts'). The current auto-extracted record (D-Glucose + O2 only) is a substrate/exchange-bound list mislabelled as a defined medium; no full formulation is definable from this paper. verdict=not_found because the paper does not describe this medium's composition. Oxygen set to aerobic: the referenced work is an aerobic E. coli glucose chemostat and the iJO1366 default simulations are aerobic (O2 exchange lower bound -18.5). No salts, N source, buffers, vitamins, or trace elements are recoverable from this paper.
evidence: Growth-associated and non-growth-associated maintenances values were recalculated based on recent E. coli K-12 MG1655 chemostat data for growth on glucose minimal media (Taymaz-Nikerel et al, 2010).

name: Minimal liquid medium + Cyc (N-epsilon-cyclopentyloxycarbonyl-L-lysine), no Trp (PMC2648862)
note: The paper only names a 'minimal liquid medium' used with an E. coli trpA amber (Trp-auxotroph) test strain; it gives NO composition (no salts, buffer, carbon/N source, vitamins, or trace elements) in the available full text — the formulation would be in Supplementary Methods, which is not in this XML. The true medium formulation therefore cannot be established or corrected. Cyc is an unnatural amino acid used for genetic-code-expansion amber suppression, not a normal medium nutrient. Also DUPLICATE of complexlit_PMC2648862_minimal_liquid_medium_cyc_no_trp (identical condition). Current entry (bare O2 + unmapped Cyc) is not a usable defined medium. Recommend removal or merge pending retrieval of Supplementary Methods.
evidence: While addition of Trp to minimal liquid medium results in wild type growth rate (2.3 h doubling time), the M. barkeri PylRS:tRNAPyl (4 h) and the DhPylRS:tRNAPyl (5.8 h) display slower but significant growth with Cyc supplementation in the absence of Trp

name: Minimal liquid medium + Cyc, no Trp (PMC2648862)
note: Same 'minimal liquid medium + Cyc, no Trp' condition as complexlit_PMC2648862_minimal_liquid_medium_cyc_n_epsilon_cyclopen (a DUPLICATE). The paper names the minimal liquid medium but provides no composition in the available full text (only Cyc supplementation of a trpA amber E. coli auxotroph is described); its formulation is in Supplementary Methods, not present in this XML. No verifiable full component list can be produced; the current entry (bare O2 + unmapped Cyc) is not a usable defined medium.
evidence: the M. barkeri PylRS:tRNAPyl (4 h) and the DhPylRS:tRNAPyl (5.8 h) display slower but significant growth with Cyc supplementation in the absence of Trp

name: Minimal liquid medium + Trp (wild-type control) (PMC2648862)
note: The paper describes adding L-Trp to a 'minimal liquid medium' as the wild-type growth control for an E. coli trpA amber (Trp-auxotroph) strain, but gives NO composition of the minimal medium (no salts, buffer, carbon/N source, vitamins, trace elements) in the available full text — it is in Supplementary Methods, not in this XML. No Trp concentration is given. The true full formulation cannot be established or corrected from the paper; the current entry (O2 + Trp only) is not a usable defined medium. Aerobic: E. coli grown with measured doubling times in liquid culture.
evidence: While addition of Trp to minimal liquid medium results in wild type growth rate (2.3 h doubling time)

name: Nutrient-rich mineral medium (Van de Waal et al. 2009), CO2/carbon-limited (PMC3160686)
note: REJECTED. This paper (Van de Waal et al. 2011, ISME J, PMC3160686) does NOT define the mineral medium composition; it only references it to a prior paper (Van de Waal et al. 2009). The extracted 'components' (O2, NaHCO3 at influx, dissolved CO2, alkalinity at influx, inflow gas pCO2) are chemostat inorganic-carbon inflow / carbon-chemistry model parameters (the DIC, alkalinity and pCO2 supply used to manipulate CO2 competition), NOT an actual growth-medium recipe. A real mineral medium would list nitrate, phosphate, Mg/Ca salts, trace metals and vitamins, none of which appear. It is a mislabelled parameter list, so it cannot stand as a defined growth medium. Organism is Microcystis aeruginosa, an oxygenic photoautotrophic freshwater cyanobacterium grown in aerated chemostats (N2 gas enriched with CO2), hence aerobic. The correct full formulation is only available in the cited Van de Waal et al. 2009 reference, not in this paper.
evidence: The chemostats were supplied with a nutrient-rich mineral medium as described by Van de Waal et al. (2009) to prevent nutrient limitation during the experiments. The mineral medium had a pH of ~8.2 in the absence of cyanobacteria.

name: not stated in main text (defined minimal medium with single carbon source; full recipe in SI Appendix Materials and Methods) (PMC12663938)
note: The main text of PMC12663938 (Zhou et al., PNAS 2025, Pseudomonas putida KT2440 glucose/ferulate metabolism) does NOT state any defined-medium composition; it explicitly defers the full recipe to the SI Appendix, which is not included in the provided full-text XML. No base salts (Na2HPO4/KH2PO4/NaCl/NH4Cl/MgSO4/CaCl2), buffer (M9/MOPS), N source, vitamins, or trace elements appear anywhere in the accessible text. The auto-extracted formulation (only glucose as 'sole carbon source' + O2) is a substrate list, not a medium, and is incomplete: the paper actually used multiple carbon sources (glucose, ferulate, acetate, succinate, benzoate). Because the true full formulation cannot be recovered or verified from the accessible paper, verdict is not_found rather than corrected. P. putida KT2440 is an obligate aerobe (proteomics/metabolomics on exponentially growing aerobic cultures), hence oxygen=aerobic. I did not invent a base composition; reconstruction would require the SI Appendix Materials and Methods.
evidence: A detailed description of the materials and methods is provided in SI Appendix. A brief description is provided here. The P. putida KT2440 cells ... were grown on either ferulate alone, glucose alone, or glucose with different gluconeogenic substrates (acetate, succinate, or ferulate).

name: Hayflick medium supplemented with 55 mM glucose (rich medium); also a chemically defined medium per Yus et al 2009 (Supplementary Table S5) (PMC3658275)
note: REJECTED as a defined-medium formulation. Hayflick medium is a COMPLEX, UNDEFINED, serum/extract-based mycoplasma medium (PPLO/beef-heart broth + serum + yeast extract, cited to Chanock et al 1962); this paper mentions it as the growth condition but never provides its composition. The paper cannot support a chemically-defined component list. The extracted entry is a single substrate (glucose) + O2 mislabelled as the full medium. The name also wrongly conflates Hayflick (rich/complex) with a SEPARATE chemically-defined medium (Yus et al 2009, Supplementary Table S5) that is described in a different paper, not in PMC3658275, and is a distinct condition, not Hayflick. I did not fabricate the Hayflick serum/extract composition, as it is not in this paper. Oxygen: M. pneumoniae is facultative/aerotolerant and consumes O2 (paper couples O2 consumption to acetate production); grown in flasks in air. Recommend removing this entry or splitting it into (a) an undefined complex medium flagged non-BiGG-mappable and (b) the Yus 2009 defined medium sourced from its own paper.
evidence: M. pneumoniae cells were grown for 48 h in 75 cm2 culture flasks with Hayflick medium supplemented with 55 mM glucose. ... In laboratory experiments, M. pneumoniae can be grown in rich and defined medium (Chanock et al, 1962; Yus et al, 2009).

name: anoxic peat soil slurry with filter-sterilized anoxic fen water (PMC4499578)
note: REJECT as a defined growth medium. This is an anoxic peat-soil-slurry mesocosm (environmental incubation), not a chemically defined or reproducible cultivation medium: the bulk base is peat soil plus filter-sterilized anoxic fen water of unknown composition, so it cannot be formulated as a component/exchange list. Only the micromolar substrate spikes (lactate/acetate/formate/propionate, 50-200 uM each) and sulfate (100-200 uM) are chemically known. CRITICAL ERROR in the current record: it is marked aerobic:true / oxygen:facultative and includes an O2 exchange component, but the paper describes a strictly anoxic, N2-gassed, butyl-septum-sealed sulfate-reduction incubation - it is anaerobic and O2 must NOT be present. The current 'lactate/acetate/formate/propionate substrate mix' collapsed under a single D-lactate exchange is also chemically wrong. Recommend removing this entry from the media database (not GEM-usable).
evidence: 30 g of soil from the 10-20-cm depth fraction were gassed in a 125-ml serum bottle with N2 and mixed under the same N2-stream with 60 ml of filter-sterilized (0.2 um), anoxic fen water. Subsequently, serum bottles were sealed with butyl rubber septa and incubated without agitation at 14 C in the dark. The soil slurries had a pH of 4. ... The mixture consisted of lactate, acetate, formate, and propionate (end-concentration 50-200 uM each). In addition, sulfate was added weekly to an end-concentration of 100-200 uM.

name: sand microcosm (4.8% gravimetric water content) contaminated with dibenzofuran (DBF) (PMC4274413)
note: This is not a chemically defined or formulable growth medium; it is a non-sterile solid-phase sand microcosm (CaCO3 content 25-55%, natural organic matter 0.028, pH 7.14, indigenous soil microbiota). Sand was contaminated with DBF (~2 mg DBF per g sand) and only moistened with a small volume of carbon-free minimal medium to reach 4.8% gravimetric water content. The heterogeneous, undefined solid matrix cannot be represented as a medium formulation. The defined liquid portion (the wetting minimal medium) is the same DSMZ 457 Brunner base WITHOUT added carbon source; the carbon (DBF) is adsorbed to sand rather than dissolved. Rejected as a substrate/microcosm mislabelled as a growth medium.
evidence: Dry sand was contaminated with DBF by spraying 10 ml of a dichloromethane/DBF solution (100 mg DBF per ml dichloromethane) on 500 g of sand ... Volumes of 25, 50, 250 and 500 ul of minimal medium were added per 1 g of sand to produce microcosms with gravimetric water content (GWC) of 2.4, 4.8, 20 and 33% ... comprehensive analysis ... in contaminated non-sterile sand.

name: milk (model resource pool) (PMC5776473)
note: PMC5776473 (Weissman et al., ISME J 2018) is a theoretical host-phage coevolution modeling paper, not an experimental media paper. The 'resource pool' is a mathematical parameter (A = 350 μg/ml of generic, chemically-undefined 'Resources' R in a Monod/chemostat ODE) with no chemical composition. 'Milk' is mentioned only once as the experimental substrate for S. thermophilus/phage 2972 serial-transfer experiments, again with no formulation provided. This extracted entry mislabels a modeling abstraction / undefined complex substrate as a defined growth medium. No true formulation exists in the paper; the current entry (O2 + placeholder 'resource pool (generic, A)') should be rejected. S. thermophilus is a facultative anaerobe (aerotolerant), hence oxygen=facultative, but this is moot since the entry is not a real medium.
evidence: "Resource pool concentration | 350 μg/ml" (Table 1); "long-term daily serial transfer experiments with S. thermophilus and its lytic phage 2972 in milk, a model system for studying CRISPR evolution"

name: Baltic Sea surface seawater + 1 umol/L DIP amendment (PMC6794295)
note: Not a defined growth medium. This is natural Baltic Sea surface seawater collected at station B1 for in-situ stable-isotope incubations, amended only with isotopic tracers (NaH13CO3, 15N2, trace 33P) and a +1 µmol/L dissolved-inorganic-phosphate spike. The paper gives no chemical formulation (no salts, N source, carbon source, vitamins, or trace elements), because the base is undefined natural seawater. The 'components' are experimental tracer/nutrient-amendment additions to a field sample, not a laboratory medium recipe. Surface, photic, actively photosynthesizing cyanobacterial bloom water => oxic/aerobic. Recommend rejection.
evidence: The third experiment (12th August 2015) was carried out as described for the first experiment with the addition of single-cell rates of CO2 and N2 fixation ... using the stable isotopes NaH13CO3 and 15N2, respectively, but under artificially increased (+1 μmol L−1) DIP concentration.

name: Baltic Sea surface seawater (in situ DIP) (PMC6794295)
note: Not a defined growth medium. Natural Baltic Sea surface seawater (station B1, 58°48'N 17°37'E, August 2015) used directly as the incubation matrix at ambient/in-situ dissolved-inorganic-phosphate concentration, with only stable-isotope tracers (NaH13CO3, 15N2, trace 33P) added for a field N2/CO2-fixation transfer experiment. No chemical composition is defined anywhere in the paper (undefined seawater base). This is an environmental sample mislabelled as a growth medium, not a formulation that can be corrected. Surface photic bloom water => oxic/aerobic. Recommend rejection.
evidence: incubations for single-cell rates of CO2 and N2 fixation by cyanobacteria and the transfer of freshly fixed C and N to their epibionts using the stable isotopes NaH13CO3 and 15N2, respectively, at in situ DIP concentrations.

name: AO medium (ammonia-oxidizer mineral medium) (PMC5600814)
note: This paper does NOT define the AO medium composition. It explicitly states the composition and preparation are 'detailed elsewhere' (reference 1 = Daims et al. 2015, Nature 528:504, the comammox Nitrospira paper). The mineral base (phosphate, salts, trace elements) is not enumerated anywhere in PMC5600814; a grep found zero salt components. The only thing this paper specifies is that NH4Cl is added as substrate (typically 1 mM NH4+) to the autoclaved mineral base, and that ammonia oxidizers are aerobic chemolithoautotrophs. The current extraction (only O2 + one uncovered 'NH4Cl') cannot be verified or completed from this source. Verdict not_found: full formulation must be taken from reference 1, not this paper. Oxygen = aerobic (aerobic ammonia oxidizers, chemolithoautotrophic).
evidence: A medium for ammonia oxidizers (AO medium), whose composition and preparation are detailed elsewhere, was used for the cultivation of ENR6, the isolation and further cultivation of N. inopinata, and the cultivation of all investigated AOA except N. viennensis. The desired concentration of NH4+ was adjusted by adding an autoclaved NH4Cl stock solution to autoclaved AO medium.

name: natural 0.2 µm-filtered surface seawater (Station ALOHA) (PMC6976679)
note: Not a defined growth medium. This is natural 0.2 µm-filtered surface seawater collected at Station ALOHA (North Pacific Subtropical Gyre) used as an undefined incubation matrix for stable-isotope tracer experiments on field-sampled Trichodesmium colonies. The paper provides NO chemical formulation (no defined salts, buffers, carbon source, or nutrient recipe) — only in-situ properties (T=27 C, salinity 34.4) and 15N/13C tracer additions for rate measurements. The auto-extracted 'components' (O2, ammonium 'in situ / incubation additions', salinity) are measured seawater properties and isotope-labelled substrate spikes mislabelled as a medium formulation, not a reproducible defined/complex growth medium. Surface seawater was oxic (60-200% air-saturation), i.e. aerobic; anaerobic N-transformations were only inferred in modelled colony microenvironments, not in the bulk incubation water. Recommend rejecting from a growth-media database as an undefined natural-seawater incubation matrix with no formulation to verify.
evidence: Colonies were individually transferred... into 5.9 ml Exetainer vials (Labco, Lampeter, UK) with 0.2 µm filtered surface seawater, adding five colonies per vial. ... Seawater temperature was 27 °C and salinity 34.4.

name: intracellular (within THP-1 human macrophages); macrophages maintained in RPMI 1640 (PMC6915324)
note: REJECT: 'intracellular (within THP-1 human macrophages)' is not a defined, reproducible growth medium. It is the intracellular host-cell niche (phagosomal/cytosolic environment) that M. tuberculosis inhabits during macrophage infection; the paper's entire premise is that Mtb scavenges undefined host-derived nitrogen (amino acids) from the macrophage, which cannot be enumerated as a chemical formulation. The only real defined medium in the paper is the macrophage MAINTENANCE medium, RPMI 1640 + 10% heat-inactivated fetal calf serum (a COMPLEX/undefined medium due to serum), and for labeling a modified RPMI + 0.2 mM L-glutamine with a single 15N amino-acid tracer per experiment. In-vitro Mtb controls were separately grown in RPMI or in 7H9 + 10% OADC. None of these describe the intracellular condition as a formulable medium. The current record's 43-component RPMI-1640 expansion (all salts, amino acids, vitamins with lower_bound uptakes) was auto-fabricated by 'base_medium_expansion' and is not stated by the paper as the organism's growth medium; the auto-extractor also flagged 'complex/undefined base' and 'fewer than 3 real components'. Organism M. tuberculosis is an obligate aerobe (aerobic), though intracellular niche can be microaerobic/hypoxic. Recommend removing this entry from a GEM-ready defined-media database.
evidence: The human monocytic THP-1 cell line was grown in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 10% heat inactivated fetal calf serum at 37C, 5% CO2 and 95% humidity. For 15N labeling experiments, modified RPMI supplemented with 0.2mM L-glutamine was used... THP-1 human macrophages were infected with Mtb in the presence of a 15N tracer... for 48 h

name: Defined lactate/sulfate chemostat medium (full composition in refs 11 and 36; not restated here) (PMC7242377)
note: REJECTED as a defined-medium entry: this paper does NOT provide a full medium formulation. It explicitly defers the composition to refs 11 and 36 ('see [11, 36] for expanded experimental details'; the entry name itself says 'full composition in refs 11 and 36; not restated here'). What was auto-extracted is only a substrate fragment (lactate electron donor + sulfate electron acceptor). Two hard errors in the current entry: (1) it lists O2 with lower_bound -10 and is flagged aerobic/facultative, but these are strictly anaerobic Desulfovibrio sulfate reducers grown under oxygen-free N2:CO2 with active anaerobic maintenance — O2 must be removed and oxygen set to anaerobic; (2) it is missing all salts, N source, buffer, vitamins, and trace elements, none of which are stated in this paper. Because the full defined recipe is not present in PMC7242377, a correct full component list cannot be produced from this source; the entry should not be served as a complete defined medium. To build a real formulation, curate from the cited primary sources (refs 11 = Leavitt et al. and 36) rather than this paper.
evidence: The growth vessel was continuously purged with high-purity, oxygen-free N2:CO2 (90:10) gas, maintaining anaerobic conditions... In all experiments, lactate was the limiting substrate and added in stoichiometric proportions to facilitate 50% sulfate consumption (see [11, 36] for expanded experimental details).

name: Continuous bioreactor fed lignocellulosic biorefinery conversion residue (complex organic substrate) (PMC7527139)
note: REJECT: this is not a chemically defined growth medium. It is a complex, undefined lignocellulosic biorefinery conversion residue (a feedstock/substrate) fed to an anaerobic mixed-culture MCFA-producing bioreactor. The paper gives no defined formulation (no salts, N source, buffers, vitamins, trace elements); the feed is only approximated as complex carbohydrate '50% xylans and 50% glucans by mass', with the detailed feedstock composition deferred to supplementary Table S2 and sourced from Scarborough et al. 2018. The auto-extracted record is also factually wrong: (1) it labels the system aerobic/facultative and includes O2, but the process is explicitly ANAEROBIC; (2) it concatenates the fermentation intermediates/products 'lactate, ethanol, acetate, CO2, H2' into a single D-lactate exchange component. These are metabolic intermediates/products of the community, not defined medium ingredients. No correct standalone medium formulation can be reconstructed from this paper.
evidence: Metabolic networks of mixed-culture fermentation that contain reactions that occur during anaerobic metabolism of a previously described lignocellulosic biorefinery residue ... iFermCell215 describes metabolism of exogenous substrates including glucans, glucose, xylans, xylose, and glycerol, along with lactate, ethanol, acetate, CO2, and H2, which are potential intermediates in this mixed-culture fermentation.

name: Artificial seawater medium (Gulf of Mexico ASM, Henson et al. 2016 [ref 80]) for phage-isolation host cultures; natural seawater-based medium for initial dilution-to-extinction host isolation (PMC8163748)
note: This paper (PMC8163748) does NOT define or describe the composition of the artificial seawater medium. It only USES it and cites its formulation to reference 80 (Henson MW et al. 2016, 'Artificial Seawater Media Facilitate Cultivating Members of the Microbial Majority from the Gulf of Mexico', mSphere, doi:10.1128/mSphere.00028-16). No salts, buffers, carbon sources, vitamins, trace elements, or concentrations appear anywhere in the main text or methods — I grepped for NaCl/MgCl/CaCl/KCl/mM/g L/carbon source/supplement/pyruvate/methanol/vitamin/trace and found nothing. The hosts (SAR11 Pelagibacter, OM43 Methylophilales) are aerobic marine heterotrophs cultivated by dilution-to-extinction in oxic seawater-based medium, so the medium is aerobic (correcting the current 'facultative' guess). The true formulation must be taken from the cited Henson et al. 2016 source, not this paper; verifying it here would require fabricating components the source paper does not contain. Current JSON has only a single O2 placeholder component plus an unmapped placeholder, i.e. no real formulation. Recommend sourcing composition from ref 80 directly and re-attributing the GrowthDB record to that DOI.
evidence: used as viral inoculum (10% v/v) in exponentially growing cultures of host bacteria in artificial seawater medium [80] in 96-well Teflon plates (Radleys, UK)

name: Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), pH 7.1 (closed system, no exogenous carbon/energy source, following wash from R2B growth medium) (PMC8379937)
note: REJECTED as a growth medium. In this paper PBS is used as a carbon/energy-free starvation/survival buffer for an extreme energy-limitation experiment, NOT a medium that supports growth. Cells were grown in R2B, washed 5x in pH 7.1 PBS to remove residual nutrients, then held in PBS with no exogenous electron donor/carbon source; populations decline over 1,000 d (net growth only from necromass recycling). The paper does not specify the PBS salt composition (only 'pH 7.1 PBS'); the components below are the standard Dulbecco-type PBS recipe given for reference, not stated in the paper. The auto-extracted record is also degenerate (only O2 plus an unmapped 'Phosphate-buffered saline' placeholder). This should not be listed as a growth medium in a GEM media database.
evidence: We discarded the supernatant and washed the cells five times with pH 7.1 phosphate buffered saline (PBS) to remove residual medium. We began the survival-curve experiment by dispensing washed cells from each taxon into repl[icate units]... in a pH-buffered solution without an external supply of electron donors... an effectively closed system with zero resource inputs over 1,000 d.

name: acidic mineral (mineral salts) medium with methane/methanol as carbon/energy source (PMC8498564)
note: PMC8498564 is a FEMS Microbiology Reviews review article on verrucomicrobial methanotrophs; it discusses environmental/physiological parameters (acidic pH, ammonium 1-28 mM, micromolar lanthanides, methane/methanol/H2 as energy sources) but does NOT define any mineral-salts-medium formulation (no g/L recipe, no salt/phosphate/trace-element table). The extracted entry is not a defined growth medium: it lists only gases/substrates (O2, CH4, CH3OH, H2) and contains zero mineral salts despite being named a 'mineral salts medium' (its own formulation_warning notes 'promises minerals, none present'). This is a substrate/energy-source list mislabeled as a medium, and the paper does not describe a mineral-salts-medium recipe to correct it to. Organism is an aerobic (O2-requiring) acidophilic methanotroph.
evidence: Methylacidiphilum fumariolicum SolV is only able to grow on methane when lanthanide-containing mud pot water (from the environment where the strain was isolated from) is added to the growth medium, or when the mineral medium contains micromolar concentrations of one or more lanthanides (Pol et al. 2014).

name: natural seawater, mesotrophic Southwest Pacific (SWP), Station 12 (PMC8776750)
note: Not a defined/formulated growth medium. This is natural seawater collected at Station 12 in the mesotrophic Southwest Pacific during the RV Sonne SO245 'UltraPac' cruise and used directly for in situ CO2-fixation incubations. The paper reports only measured field properties of this water (salinity >34.5 PSU, DIN, DOC ~80-107 umol/L, chlorophyll a), never a reproducible recipe with weighable components. The auto-extracted 'components' (O2, salinity, DOC) are environmental descriptors, not medium ingredients. An undefined natural-seawater sample mislabelled as a growth medium should be rejected for a media database. (Nutrient-amendment experiments added only 1 uM NH4/NO3, 0.2 uM PO4, or 2 nM Fe to the same natural seawater as perturbations, which also does not constitute a defined medium.)
evidence: the mesotrophic waters of the SWP (Stations 11 to 15) along the southwestern periphery of the SPG

name: natural seawater, oligotrophic South Pacific Gyre core, Station 8 (surface ~20 m and chl a max) (PMC8776750)
note: Not a defined/formulated growth medium. This is natural seawater from the ultraoligotrophic South Pacific Gyre core (Station 8, surface ~20 m and deep chlorophyll a max), sampled by CTD and used directly for 13CO2-fixation and single-cell nanoSIMS incubations. The paper describes only field-measured properties (salinity >35 PSU, DIN <0.1 umol/L, DOC >80 umol/L, chlorophyll a max), not a reproducible list of chemical components. The auto-extracted entries (O2, chlorophyll a, DIN, DOC, salinity) are environmental parameters, not medium ingredients, and 'chlorophyll a (surface)' as a medium component is an extraction artifact. An undefined natural-seawater environmental sample cannot serve as a curated growth-medium formulation and should be rejected.
evidence: the oligotrophic waters of the SPG (Stations 2 through 10) ... DIN concentrations (<0.1 umol l-1) ... DOC concentrations were >80 umol l-1 throughout the core SPG

name: Anaerobic biogas digestate (municipal wastewater sludge, poly-aluminum-chloride/ferric-chloride precipitated) amended with N2O in a He headspace (PMC8776835)
note: This is a complex, chemically UNDEFINED substrate (raw anaerobic biogas digestate from municipal wastewater sludge), not a defined growth medium. Bacteria were enriched/grown by adding N2O to the intact digestate under a He headspace, tapping into fermentation intermediates produced by the resident methanogenic consortium. The paper gives only bulk characterization (~2.1% organic matter, 1.8-1.9 g NH4+-N/L, 16-32 Meq VFA/L, pH 7.6-8.2, He+N2O headspace with ~5 mM N2O in liquid); no chemically defined salts/vitamins/trace-element recipe and no specified carbon source exist. It therefore cannot be represented as a component list or mapped to BiGG exchanges as a GEM-ready medium. Rejected as 'not a real defined growth medium' (undefined complex digestate mislabeled as a defined medium). Isolate purification used unspecified agar plates of 'different media composition' incubated anaerobically with N2O, again not a defined formulation in the main text.
evidence: The sludge was a poly-aluminum chloride (PAX-XL61, Kemira) and ferric chloride (PIX318, Kemira) precipitated municipal wastewater sludge, with an organic matter content of 5.6% (w/w). Both digestors reduced the organic matter by approximately 60%, producing digestates containing ~2.1 % organic matter, 1.8-1.9 g NH4+-N L-1, ~16 and 32 Meq VFA L-1, pH = 7.6-7.8 and 8.2; mesophilic and thermophilic, respectively.

name: M9 minimal medium (single-carbon-source variants) (PMC8546961)
note: REJECT as a single medium. This record lumps ALL SIX carbon sources (citrate, fructose, glucose, gluconate, glycerol, ribose) into one medium, but the paper's own wording ('...or 20 mM gluconate') makes them MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE alternatives — each defines a SEPARATE M9 single-carbon-source variant (the record's own name even says 'single-carbon-source variants'). P. putida KT2440 was never grown on a 6-carbon cocktail; each culture used exactly one carbon source normalized to 120 mM total carbon (20 mM citrate/glucose/fructose/gluconate x 6C, 24 mM ribose x 5C, 40 mM glycerol x 3C). A combined all-six medium is not a real defined growth medium in this paper. The correct action is to split into six separate M9 + single-carbon media (M9+glucose 20 mM, M9+fructose 20 mM, M9+gluconate 20 mM, M9+glycerol 40 mM, M9+citrate 20 mM, M9+ribose 24 mM); the ribose variant already exists as the other record. Components below list the shared M9 base plus the six ALTERNATIVE carbon sources for reference only — they must not coexist in one medium. Also: oxygen is aerobic (obligate aerobe), not facultative.
evidence: M9 minimal medium (24) supplemented with 20 mM citrate, 20 mM glucose, 24 mM ribose, 20 mM fructose, 40 mM glycerol, or 20 mM gluconate was used for phenotypic characterization of P. putida KT2440.

name: glucose (bioconversion feedstock) (PMC9335188)
note: PMC9335188 is a PNAS techno-economic/life-cycle analysis paper (doi:10.1073/pnas.2122309119) comparing in-planta accumulation vs microbial production of bioproducts (4-HBA, catechol, muconic acid, PDC). Glucose is treated purely as an industrial bioconversion FEEDSTOCK/carbon source in a process model, NOT as part of any defined growth medium. The paper explicitly declines to specify medium components ('Other nutrients are provided based on medium requirements of host organisms'). No salts, N source, buffers, vitamins, or trace elements are given. This is a substrate mislabelled as a growth medium, so it is rejected rather than corrected. The two auto-extracted components (D-glucose + O2) do not constitute a real, paper-defined growth medium.
evidence: In microbial systems, sugar (pure glucose) is used as the carbon source to produce bioproducts. Other nutrients are provided based on medium requirements of host organisms for each bioproduct.

name: Synthetic Feed-in-Time (FIT) fed-batch medium (m2p-labs) + EnzMix (PMC9561276)
note: REJECTED as a defined growth-medium formulation. The FIT fed-batch medium is a proprietary commercial product from m2p-labs (BioLector microbioreactor cultivation of E. coli, aerobic, 48-well Flowerplates, 1400 rpm, gas-permeable films). This paper does NOT disclose its composition — it cites Fink et al. (ref 45) for the method and names only the carbon sources (glucose + dextran) plus the glucoamylase EnzMix. No salts, buffer, nitrogen source, MgSO4/CaCl2, vitamins, or trace elements are given, so a full, correct, reproducible component list cannot be reconstructed from this paper. The auto-extracted entry is a carbon-source/substrate list mislabeled as a full medium (its own metadata flags 'fewer than 3 real components' and 'formulation not manually verified'). Not BiGG-mappable as a complete minimal-medium definition. Oxygen is aerobic (shaken microbioreactor, gas-permeable sealing).
evidence: an aliquot of 50 μl was inoculated into a 750 μl synthetic Feed in Time (FIT) fed-batch medium containing glucose and dextran as carbon sources (m2p-labs). Immediately before inoculation, 1% (v/v) of the glucose-releasing enzyme mix (EnzMix) was added. The hydrolytic enzyme (glucoamylase) cleaves off glucose residues from solubilized glucose polymer dextran.

name: BA (basal anaerobic) medium with H2/CH4/CO2 gas feed (PMC9347119)
note: The paper mentions BA (Basal Anaerobic) medium is used for a hydrogenotrophic CO2-methanation community but does NOT give its chemical formulation in the text. The composition (ions, trace elements, buffer, amounts) is deferred entirely to Supplementary Dataset S5 (MOESM6, an .xlsx of ModelSEED IDs) and to external reference [40] (Angelidaki, Petersen & Ahring, Appl Microbiol Biotechnol 1990). No salts, buffer, trace-element, or amount values are recoverable from the article full text, so a verified full component list cannot be built from this source and I will not invent the Angelidaki BA salts. Separately, the current auto-extracted record is factually wrong: it lists 'input gas CH4' as a feed component, but CH4 is the PRODUCT of methanation, not a feed. The FBA feed was CO2 + H2 fixed at a 1:4 ratio (CO2 uptake 1.20, H2 uptake 5.11 mmol/gDW/h) with CH4 as a minimum export (1.15 mmol/gDW/h). Oxygen = anaerobic is correct (anaerobic flasks, hydrogenotrophic methanogens/syntrophs). To formulate this medium correctly, retrieve Supplementary Dataset S5 or Angelidaki et al. 1990 [ref 40].
evidence: Samples collected from the four reactors were inoculated and maintained in anaerobic flasks with Basal Anaerobic medium (BA) using CO2 as carbon source [18]. ... an in silico basic anaerobic (BA) medium was defined based on its measured composition (Supplementary Dataset S5). ... The concentration of single ions and metabolites was calculated from the BA composition protocol ... [40].

name: SDMM (semi-defined minimal medium) (PMC9519441)
note: This paper NAMES and USES SDMM but does NOT define its composition — it cites the recipe to reference 2 (Jensen, Zhu & van Opijnen, Cell Rep 2017). The only quantitative component the paper itself states is 20 mM glucose as carbon source; no salts, buffers, amino acids, vitamins, or trace elements are given, and 'semi-defined' means the base contains undefined complex components. Therefore the full true formulation cannot be reconstructed from THIS paper without fabrication. Two corrections to the current stub regardless: (1) oxygen — cultures grown statically in 5% CO2 with oxyrase (an enzymatic O2 scavenger), so the condition is oxygen-depleted/anaerobic, NOT facultative-aerobic; the O2 exchange should not be an available uptake. (2) The entry is not a genuine defined-medium formulation (2 components, glucose + O2) and cannot be verified against this source. Recommend either sourcing the composition from ref 2 (Cell Rep 2017, PMID 28813680) or removing/flagging the entry. Marked not_found because the paper does not describe the medium's composition.
evidence: Liquid cultures were grown statically in THY, C+Y or semi-defined minimal media (SDMM) at pH 7.3, with 5 µl ml-1 oxyrase (Oxyrase) ... diluted to an OD600 of 0.1 in SDMM, with 20 mM glucose as carbon source.

name: Bathypelagic seawater + 1 uM thiosulfate + 10 uM glucose + 10 uM acetate (thiosulfate + DOM) (PMC10625248)
note: REJECTED as a defined growth medium. This is natural, undefined Labrador Sea Water (~2000 m bathypelagic seawater) incubated with its native mixed prokaryotic community and merely spiked with trace (µM) amendments for a metatranscriptomic amendment experiment — not a chemically defined, reproducible culture medium. The seawater's bulk ionic composition (Na+, Cl-, Mg2+, SO4 2-, Ca2+, K+, HCO3-, natural DOM, nutrients) is never defined in the paper, so a GEM-ready formulation cannot be reconstructed. The auto-extracted components are also incomplete and partly artifactual: 'Sodium' (1 µM) and 'Sulfate' (1 µM) arise from salt-dissociation of Na2S2O3 rather than from any stated medium salt, and no real medium base exists. Oxygen: aerobic (the study explicitly concerns the 'oxygenated bathypelagic realm'). Recommend removing this record from the defined-media database, or reclassifying it as an environmental incubation/amendment treatment rather than a culture medium.
evidence: The water was split in duplicate 2 L acid-cleaned polycarbonate bottles and amended with 10 µM ammonia, 1 µM sulfite (Na2SO3), 1 µM thiosulfate (Na2S2O3), 1 µM thiosulfate and 10 µM of each glucose and acetate (thiosulfate + DOM treatment). All the treatments together with an unamended control were incubated at in situ temperature in the dark for 1 h or 72 h.

name: BS6-derived cellobiose enrichment medium (exact substrate for this doubling-time measurement not specified in text) (PMC11129491)
note: The paper defines no separate 'BS6-derived cellobiose enrichment medium'; the record's own name concedes 'exact substrate ... not specified in text'. It is a spurious duplicate of BS6 (same cellobiose composition) generated from a growth-rate/doubling-time mention, and it is also internally inconsistent (Chloride listed as 20 g/L, Calcium dropped, O2 wrongly present). Not an independently defined medium -> reject; use the corrected BS6 record instead.
evidence: Growth medium 6, 7, 8, and 9 (BS6-9) were prepared by replacing the cellulose in the BS2 medium by cellobiose (0.2 g L−1) ...

name: BS-series defined medium (glucose/pyruvate/acetate utilized; substrate for this specific doubling-time measurement not specified) (PMC11129491)
note: No 'BS-series defined medium' with the composition given (Cl 20, K 0.5, Mg 3.6, SO4 4.3, Na 20, no carbon source, no Ca, O2 present) is defined in the paper. The record's own name admits the substrate is 'not specified'. It is a hallucinated composite assembled from substrate-utilization statements (glucose/pyruvate/acetate), not a defined medium. The real defined single-substrate media are BS6-9. Reject.
evidence: members of the phylum Desulfobacterota ... utilized structurally less complex carbon substrates, such as lactate, pyruvate, and acetate (Table S5).

name: Defined marine anaerobic medium (BS-type) with lactate/pyruvate/acetate as substrate (PMC11129491)
note: The paper defines no lactate-containing 'defined marine anaerobic (BS-type) medium'. Lactate/pyruvate/acetate appear only as a substrate-utilization finding for Pseudodesulfovibrio S3T/S3-i (Table S5); those strains were actually isolated on BS8 (pyruvate). The record is a hallucinated medium conflating a substrate list with a formulation (partial BS1-strength salts, D-lactate with no amount). Correctly anaerobic, but not a defined medium -> reject.
evidence: Desulfobacterota (Pseudodesulfovibrio sp. strains S3T and S3-i) utilized structurally less complex carbon substrates, such as lactate, pyruvate, and acetate ... Lactate was their preferred substrate, which can be oxidized to pyruvate by lactate dehydrogenase.

name: Basal medium + sugar (glucose, xylose, or cellobiose; concentration not stated), high-throughput screen (PMC11575207)
note: REJECT as a distinct medium: this entry is an ambiguous OR-list of substrates ('glucose, xylose, or cellobiose; concentration not stated') collapsed into one record with NO carbon source actually captured — it is not a single, defined, growth-supporting medium. The three concrete conditions it enumerates are already the separately-defined media growthlit_PMC11575207_basal_medium_10_g_l_glucose, _10_g_l_xylose, and _cellobiose_microplate_optim (this entry is redundant with them). The only genuine no-sugar variant the paper uses is the overnight seed culture ('basal medium containing no sugar'), which grows only weakly on yeast-extract carbon and is not a fermentation medium. Basal-medium composition (for reference only) is given below; note it also omitted the 1.5 g/L yeast extract and 100 mM sodium MES buffer.
evidence: Growth of B-768 on various carbon sources (glucose, xylose, cellobiose, galactose, mannose, or arabinose) ... B-768 was inoculated in basal medium containing no sugar and grown at 50°C overnight.

name: Filtered Goodenough Lake water (alkaline soda lake) spiked with ~2% NaH13CO3 (PMC12598745)
note: Not a defined/formulated growth medium. This is filtered NATURAL alkaline soda-lake water (Goodenough Lake) spiked with a ~2% NaH13CO3 isotopic tracer for in situ 13C-carbon-fixation probing of intact microbial mats. The paper provides no defined recipe for this water in the main text (only a 'mimicked 2018 Goodenough Lake water' in Supplementary Table 9, used to make GC-MS calibration standards, not as the incubation medium), so it cannot be reconstructed as a laboratory medium. The auto-extracted composition is also wrong: 'Sodium 14 g/L' and 'Chloride 14 g/L' were both mis-derived from a BIOMASS WASH buffer ('2 ml 14 g/1000 ml NaCl, similar to the salinity of Goodenough Lake'), not the medium, and 14 g/L NaCl cannot give 14 g/L of each ion; ammonium/nitrate 'little to none (trace)' were taken from Introduction prose about the lake; and the defining component of soda-lake water and of this experiment (~750 mM dissolved inorganic carbon as CO2/HCO3-/CO3(2-) plus the NaH13CO3 spike) is entirely absent. Recommend removing from the database.
evidence: Intact microbial mats were incubated with a filtered Goodenough Lake water spiked with ~2% NaH13CO3 to trace carbon incorporation over a 48-h period

name: Universal minimal salts (UMS) (PMC8324050)
note: The paper does NOT state the composition of UMS; it references the recipe to citation 61 (Poole) and only lists supplements. Therefore the UMS salt base (phosphate salts, MgSO4, CaCl2, trace elements, Fe, vitamins, etc.) cannot be sourced from THIS paper, and I will not invent amounts. The current extracted components are a mis-extraction of the A. caulinodans supplement sentence (nicotinate 0.3 mM belongs there; the accompanying 10 mM succinate was dropped) with spurious Na+/Cl- salt-dissociation artifacts; they do not represent UMS. Organisms (Rhizobium leguminosarum bv. viciae 3841 / Azorhizobium caulinodans ORS571) are aerobic rhizobia grown aerobically when free-living (diazotrophic A. caulinodans sparged with 3% O2). Components below are only the paper-stated supplements for the A. caulinodans variant, shown for transparency; the medium's full formulation is not defined in this paper.
evidence: Cultures were grown in universal minimal salts (UMS) (61) or acid minimal salts (AMS) (63) medium at 28C. ... A. caulinodans ORS571 was grown in UMS medium supplemented with 10 mM succinate and 0.3 mM nicotinic acid at 37C.

name: UMS (universal minimal salts) supplemented (PMC8324050)
note: The extracted entry lumps mutually EXCLUSIVE alternative carbon sources (succinate 20 mM, arabinose 20 mM, glucose 10 mM) plus the nitrogen source NH4Cl 10 mM into a single medium. The paper lists these as separate supplements used in different experiments, never as one combined medium, so the extracted formulation is not a real defined growth medium. In addition, the UMS salt base is not given in this paper (cited to reference 61) and is absent from the entry. It cannot be corrected into a valid single medium from this paper without inventing both the salt base and an arbitrary choice of one carbon source. Rhizobia here are aerobic free-living cultures. Components listed are the paper-stated supplements (as documentation only); they are alternatives, not a co-present recipe.
evidence: UMS was supplemented with different carbon and nitrogen sources at the following final concentrations: succinate, 20 mM; arabinose, 20 mM; glucose, 10 mM; and NH4Cl, 10 mM.

name: Synthetic minimal medium (SMM) with substituted carbon sources (PMC12172418)
note: This entry is a substrate/carbon-source panel mislabelled as a growth medium, so it is rejected as a usable medium formulation. Two independent problems: (1) The 13 components are exactly the paper's Table 2 ('Testing of different carbon sources') — 12 sugars plus O2. Table 2 is NOT a medium; the paper states each sugar was substituted for D-glucose ONE AT A TIME ('substituting D-glucose with alternative sugars in amounts adjusted for carbon content', 'different amounts of other sugars systematically replaced glucose'). Bundling all 12 mutually-exclusive glucose substitutes into a single co-present formulation describes no experiment the paper ever ran. (2) The real base — the Synthetic Minimal Medium (SMM, from ref 30, Hussain/Kohler chemically defined medium for S. aureus) — is entirely absent. The paper puts the full SMM composition (inorganic salts and ions, amino acids, vitamins including nicotinamide substituted for nicotinic acid, plus the added trace metals) in Table S1 (supplementary) and only summarizes: 'The concentrations of the various components served as lower bounds ... as detailed in Table S1. In addition to the already provided salts and ions, we added minimal traces of zinc (EX_zn2_e), cobalt (EX_cobalt2_e), and copper (EX_cu2_e).' Table S1 is not present in the fulltext XML, so the correct SMM salts/amino-acids/vitamins cannot be reconstructed from this paper alone. Because the object neither represents a real single-experiment medium nor contains the SMM base, it should be rejected (or rebuilt from Table S1) rather than 'corrected'. Note also a likely extraction bug: the paper's 'D-arabinose' is mapped in Table 2 to EX_arab__L_e (L-arabinose) at lower bound -4.15, and the JSON inherited L-Arabinose. Oxygen: paper set EX_o2_e lower bound to -20 mmol/gDW/h (aerobic simulation); S. epidermidis is itself a facultative anaerobe.
evidence: For the growth simulations in different carbon sources, we used the SMM with nicotinamide instead of nicotinic acid as a basis (Table 2). The concentrations reported in the medium were established as lower bounds for the simulation. The concentrations of the listed carbon sources were calculated to be equivalent in carbon content to the initial 5 g/L of glucose used in the defined SMM.

name: Diluted whey (DW) medium, initial pH adjusted to 7.0 (PMC2896665)
note: Not a chemically defined medium. 'Diluted whey (DW)' is a natural dairy substrate (cheese whey diluted with water). Its reported composition consists only of bulk analytical parameters (total nitrogen, total phosphorus, proteins) plus lactose, which cannot be reconstituted as a defined formulation and are not BiGG-mappable species. Rejected as a natural-substrate list per the verification rules. Reported values are documented in components for the record; paper confirms lactose 22.0 g/L, total N 0.45 g/L, total P 0.25 g/L, proteins 2.0 g/L, native pH 5.4 (fermentation initial pH adjusted to 7.0). Grown aerobically (E. faecium CECT 410).
evidence: The DW medium (2.7% dry weight after water evaporation) used as fermentation medium contained (in g/L): lactose, 22.0; total nitrogen, 0.45; total phosphorus, 0.25; proteins, 2.0; pH, 5.4. Culture conditions were aerobic (30 C, 200 rpm, 0.5 L/h aeration; initial pH adjusted to 7.0).

name: Realkalized fed-batch fermentation I: DW medium (initial pH 7.0) fed with 400 g/L concentrated lactose (PMC2896665)

The base is a natural substrate defined only by bulk analytical parameters, so it is rejected on the same grounds as the DW record. Values are documented for the record. Grown aerobically.
evidence: Realkalized fed-batch fermentation I was fed with a 400 g/L concentrated lactose and sterile distilled water (if needed), over the DW medium (lactose 22.0; total nitrogen 0.45; total phosphorus 0.25; proteins 2.0 g/L). Aerobic (30 C, 200 rpm, 0.5 L/h aeration).

name: Realkalized fed-batch fermentation II: DW medium (initial pH 7.0) fed with a mixture of 400 g/L concentrated lactose and concentrated whey (CW) medium (PMC2896665)
